title: Supply: the curve, shifts, and determinants

questions.explanation: The upward slope comes from rising marginal cost: producers expand output while price covers the cost of the next unit, and in the short run each additional unit tends to cost more (overtime, less-efficient capacity). Higher prices therefore make more units worth producing. 'Sellers like high prices' isn't a mechanism, and demand doesn't enter the supply curve.

Only a change in the good's own price moves you along its supply curve — that's a change in quantity supplied. Cheaper inputs, better technology, and more sellers all change how much is offered at every price, so they shift the whole curve (a change in supply).

Corn and soybeans are substitutes in production — the same land can grow either. When soybean prices rise, planting corn now means giving up more soybean revenue, so acreage migrates and corn supply shifts left. It's a shift (the corn price didn't change), and it's a supply-side story, not a demand one.
Qs = −100 + 30(9) = −100 + 270 = 170 loaves. The slope coefficient means each extra dollar of price draws out 30 more loaves once the market is past its entry price.

Set Qs = 0: −100 + 30P = 0 → P = 100/30 ≈ $3.33. The negative intercept encodes an entry threshold — below this price not even the lowest-cost producer covers the marginal cost of its first units, so market quantity is zero.

A per-unit tax on sellers shifts the supply curve up by exactly the tax: for any quantity, sellers must receive a price $2 higher to net what that quantity required before. It's a parallel vertical displacement (equivalently a left shift), not a rotation — the tax is the same $2 on every unit.

Produce every batch whose marginal cost is at or below price: $4, $6, $9, and $13 all pass at P = $13 (the 4th batch exactly breaks even on the margin), but the 5th costs $18 > $13, so it's not produced. Quantity supplied = 4 — reading the MC schedule as the firm's supply curve.
